questions.stem: The elementary charge is $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. How many electrons make up a total charge of magnitude $3.2 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$?
questions.explanation: Charge is quantised in units of $e$, so the number of electrons is $n = Q/e = (3.2 \times 10^{-19})/(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 2$. Charge can only come in whole multiples of $e$, so $3.2$ as a count is impossible. The value $0.5$ inverts the ratio, dividing $e$ by $Q$. The trap is $1$, which assumes a single electron without comparing the given charge to one elementary charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A plastic comb gains a charge of $-4.8 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{C}$ after being rubbed on hair. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, how many electrons did the comb gain?
questions.explanation: The number of excess electrons is $n = |Q|/e = (4.8 \times 10^{-18})/(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 30$. Watch the powers of ten: $10^{-18}/10^{-19} = 10^{1}$, so $4.8/1.6 = 3$ becomes $30$. Dropping that factor of ten gives the false $3$, and adding an extra factor gives $300$. The trap is $48$, which reads off the digits of the charge without dividing by $e$ at all.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: When charging by friction, electrons are transferred between two neutral bodies. Which statement is correct?
options: Protons move from one body to the other to share the charge.
Both bodies become negative because electrons are added to the system.
The body that gains electrons becomes positive.
The body that gains electrons becomes negative and the body that loses them becomes positive.
questions.explanation: Friction transfers only electrons, which carry negative charge. The body collecting extra electrons ends up negative, and the body left short of electrons is positive by the same amount, so charge is conserved overall. No electrons are created, so both cannot become negative. Protons are bound in nuclei and do not move during ordinary charging. The trap is the option saying a body that gains electrons becomes positive, which reverses the sign of the electron's charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A neutral metal sphere is touched by a rod carrying a charge of $+8\ \text{nC}$, and after contact the rod and sphere are identical in size and share the charge equally. What charge remains on the sphere?
questions.explanation: Two identical conductors in contact share the total charge equally. The total is $+8\ \text{nC}$, so each ends up with $+8/2 = +4\ \text{nC}$. Charging by conduction leaves the sphere with the same sign as the rod, so a negative answer is wrong. The sphere does not stay neutral, since contact transfers real charge. The trap is $+8\ \text{nC}$, which forgets that the rod keeps half of the charge rather than handing all of it over.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charged rod with a negative charge is brought near, but not touching, one end of an isolated neutral metal bar. What is the sign of the charge induced on the near end of the bar?
questions.explanation: The negative rod repels the bar's free electrons, pushing them to the far end. The near end is then left short of electrons, so it is positive, while the far end becomes negative. The bar as a whole stays neutral because no charge enters or leaves it; the charge is only redistributed. The trap is the negative option, which forgets that like charges repel and so wrongly draws electrons toward the rod rather than away from it.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two identical metal spheres carry charges of $+12\ \mu\text{C}$ and $-4\ \mu\text{C}$. They are touched together and then separated. What is the charge on each sphere afterwards?
questions.explanation: The total charge is conserved: $+12 + (-4) = +8\ \mu\text{C}$. Because the spheres are identical, they share this equally, so each carries $+8/2 = +4\ \mu\text{C}$. Using $+8\ \mu\text{C}$ forgets to halve the shared total, and $+16\ \mu\text{C}$ adds the magnitudes instead of accounting for the opposite signs. The trap is $-4\ \mu\text{C}$, which keeps the original charges in place rather than redistributing the net charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge of $1\ \text{C}$ is enormous on the laboratory scale. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, how many electrons carry a total charge of magnitude $1\ \text{C}$?
questions.explanation: The count is $n = Q/e = 1/(1.6 \times 10^{-19})$. Since $1/1.6 = 0.625$, this is $0.625 \times 10^{19} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}$ electrons. The answer $1.6 \times 10^{19}$ misreads the reciprocal of $1.6$, and the negative exponents quote $e$ itself rather than its reciprocal. The trap is $1.6 \times 10^{19}$, which keeps $1.6$ instead of inverting it to $0.625$ when dividing.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A copper wire carries a steady current that delivers a charge of $90\ \text{C}$ past a point. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, how many electrons pass that point? Give the answer to two significant figures.
questions.explanation: The number of electrons is $n = Q/e = 90/(1.6 \times 10^{-19})$. Compute $90/1.6 = 56.25$, so $n = 56.25 \times 10^{19} = 5.6 \times 10^{20}$. Writing $5.6 \times 10^{19}$ forgets that $56.25 \times 10^{19}$ must be renormalised by one power of ten. The value $1.8 \times 10^{21}$ multiplies $90$ by $2 \times 10^{19}$ by rounding $e$ badly. The trap is $1.4 \times 10^{19}$, which divides $e$ into $90$ upside down and then mishandles the exponent.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Charge is a conserved quantity. In a closed, isolated system, which statement is always true?
options: The total positive charge alone stays constant.
The total electric charge stays constant.
The total number of electrons stays constant.
Every object in the system keeps its own charge fixed.
questions.explanation: Conservation of charge says the algebraic sum of all charges in an isolated system is fixed, even though charge can move from one body to another. The number of electrons can change in processes such as ionisation, so that count is not conserved. Positive charge alone is not separately conserved, since positive and negative can be created or cancelled in equal amounts. The trap is the last option, which confuses conservation of the total with the much stronger and false claim that no charge ever moves between objects.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A glass rod rubbed with silk acquires a charge of $+5\ \text{nC}$. What is the charge left on the silk, assuming the rod and silk started neutral and formed an isolated system?
questions.explanation: The pair started neutral, so by conservation of charge their charges must still sum to zero. If the rod is $+5\ \text{nC}$, the silk must be $-5\ \text{nC}$. The rod became positive by losing electrons, which the silk gained, making the silk negative. A reading of $+5\ \text{nC}$ would double the system's charge from zero, and $0\ \text{nC}$ ignores the electrons that moved. The trap is $-10\ \text{nC}$, which doubles the transferred charge instead of matching the rod's $+5\ \text{nC}$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A small oil drop carries an excess charge that is found to be $-8.0 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, how many excess electrons are on the drop?
questions.explanation: By Millikan's quantisation result, charges on drops are integer multiples of $e$. Here $n = |Q|/e = (8.0 \times 10^{-19})/(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 5$. The figure $8$ reads off the digits of the charge directly, ignoring the divide by $1.6$. The value $10$ comes from using $e/2$ by mistake. The trap is $4$, which rounds $8/1.6 = 5$ down as though the answer were $8/2$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Which of the following could NOT be the net charge on an isolated object, given that the elementary charge is $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$?
questions.explanation: Net charge must be a whole-number multiple of $e$. Dividing each option by $e$ gives $1.5$, $1$, $2$, and $3$ respectively. Only $2.4 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ gives a fraction ($1.5$), so it cannot exist as a free charge. The others are exactly $1e$, $2e$, and $3e$. The trap is to assume any small charge is allowed, but quantisation forbids the half-integer value $1.5e$.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two identical neutral conducting spheres, A and B, are mounted on insulating stands. A negatively charged rod is held near sphere A while A and B are momentarily in contact, then A and B are separated, and finally the rod is removed. What are the signs of the charges on A and B?
options: Both A and B are negative
A is positive and B is negative
Both A and B stay neutral
A is negative and B is positive
questions.explanation: This is charging by induction with a shared pair. The negative rod repels free electrons from A (the near sphere) onto B (the far sphere). With the spheres separated before the rod leaves, A is left electron-poor and so positive, while B holds the extra electrons and is negative. The signs are opposite, so both-negative and both-neutral are wrong. The trap is the reversed option, A negative and B positive, which sends electrons toward the repelling rod instead of away from it.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An object has $4.0 \times 10^{20}$ protons and $4.0 \times 10^{20} + 5.0 \times 10^{12}$ electrons. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, what is the net charge on the object?
questions.explanation: Only the imbalance matters. There are $5.0 \times 10^{12}$ more electrons than protons, so the net charge is $Q = -(5.0 \times 10^{12})(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = -8.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C}$. The sign is negative because electrons are in excess. Treating the exponents as $10^{12} \times 10^{-19} = 10^{-8}$ instead of $10^{-7}$ gives the wrong magnitude $8.0 \times 10^{-8}$. A positive sign reverses the excess, and zero ignores the imbalance. The trap is $+8.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C}$, right in size but wrong in sign.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A balloon rubbed on a wool jumper sticks to a wall, even though the wall is electrically neutral. What is the best explanation?
options: The wall must already carry a charge opposite to the balloon.
The charged balloon polarises the wall, inducing an opposite charge on the wall's surface, and the attraction holds it there.
The balloon transfers all its charge to the wall on contact.
Like charges on the balloon and wall attract each other.
questions.explanation: A charged body attracts a neutral one by induction. The balloon's charge polarises the molecules in the wall, pulling opposite charge slightly closer to the surface, and since that induced charge is nearer it produces a net attraction. The wall need not be pre-charged. Like charges repel, never attract, so that option is wrong on the basic rule. The trap is the contact option: the balloon sticks without giving up its charge, since induction needs no charge transfer.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charge of $-1.0\ \mu\text{C}$ is added to a metal sphere that was already neutral. By how much does the mass of the sphere change? Take the electron mass as $9.1 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$ and $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.
questions.explanation: Negative charge means added electrons, and each electron has mass, so the sphere gets slightly heavier. The number of electrons is $n = Q/e = (1.0 \times 10^{-6})/(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 6.25 \times 10^{12}$, and the mass added is $n m_e = (6.25 \times 10^{12})(9.1 \times 10^{-31}) \approx 5.7 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{kg}$. The mass cannot decrease, because electrons are added, not removed. The single-electron mass $9.1 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$ forgets there are billions of them. The trap is the no-change option, which assumes charge is massless.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An electroscope has a negatively charged metal leaf and stem, so the leaf stands deflected. A positively charged rod is brought close to the metal cap without touching it. What happens to the leaf deflection?
options: The leaf falls and then deflects on the opposite side immediately.
It stays exactly the same, because the rod does not touch the cap.
It increases, because bringing any charge nearer adds to the deflection.
It decreases, because the positive rod draws electrons up toward the cap and away from the leaf.
questions.explanation: The leaf deflects because excess electrons spread over the stem and leaf, repelling each other. A nearby positive rod attracts some of those electrons up to the cap, thinning the charge on the leaf, so the deflection decreases. Induction works without contact, so the no-change claim is wrong. Bringing a positive charge near a negative system reduces, rather than adds to, the leaf's charge. The trap is the increase option, which ignores that the rod's sign is opposite to the leaf's charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A conductor and an insulator are each given the same small amount of excess charge. Which statement correctly contrasts them?
options: On the conductor the charge stays put, while on the insulator it spreads freely.
Neither can hold any excess charge at all.
On both, the charge spreads uniformly through the entire volume.
On the conductor the charge spreads over the whole surface, while on the insulator it stays where it was placed.
questions.explanation: A conductor has free electrons, so excess charge moves until it reaches equilibrium on the outer surface, spreading out under mutual repulsion. An insulator has bound charges, so excess charge stays essentially where it was deposited. Charge resides on the surface of a conductor, not throughout its volume, so the volume option is wrong. Both materials can certainly hold excess charge. The trap is the reversed option, which swaps the behaviours of conductor and insulator.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An ion of magnesium, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, has lost two electrons relative to the neutral atom. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, what is the net charge of this ion?
questions.explanation: Losing two electrons leaves the atom with two more protons than electrons, a net charge of $+2e = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = +3.2 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. The sign is positive because electrons were removed. A single $e$ counts only one missing electron, and $+6.4 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ corresponds to four missing electrons. The trap is the negative version, correct in size but wrong in sign, since removing electrons gives a positive ion.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two identical metal spheres carry charges of $+10\ \text{nC}$ and $+2\ \text{nC}$. They are brought into contact and then separated. By how much did the charge on the second sphere change?
questions.explanation: The total charge is $+10 + 2 = +12\ \text{nC}$, shared equally to give $+6\ \text{nC}$ on each sphere. The second sphere went from $+2\ \text{nC}$ to $+6\ \text{nC}$, an increase of $4\ \text{nC}$. The figure $6\ \text{nC}$ quotes the final charge, not the change. A decrease is wrong because the second sphere ended up more charged, and it certainly did change. The trap is the no-change option, which forgets that contact between conductors redistributes charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A charged sphere carries a charge of $-4.8 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{C}$. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, find the number of excess electrons and the sign of the charge.
questions.solution: The number of excess electrons is $n = |Q|/e = (4.8 \times 10^{-18})/(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = $ {{1}} electrons. The sphere has an excess of electrons, so its charge is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Dividing $4.8/1.6 = 3$ and $10^{-18}/10^{-19} = 10^{1}$ gives $n = 30$. Excess electrons always mean a negative charge.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: Two identical metal spheres carry charges of $+12\ \mu\text{C}$ and $-4\ \mu\text{C}$. They are touched together and then separated. Find the final charge on each sphere.
questions.solution: By conservation of charge the total is $12 + (-4) = $ {{1}} microcoulombs. The spheres are identical, so they share equally, giving each a final charge of {{2}} microcoulombs.
questions.explanation: The net charge is $+8\ \mu\text{C}$, and halving it gives $+4\ \mu\text{C}$ on each. Opposite signs partly cancel, so the total is found by adding signed values, not magnitudes.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A wire delivers a charge of $1.0\ \text{C}$ past a point. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, find the number of electrons and state the SI unit of charge.
questions.solution: The number of electrons is $n = Q/e = 1/(1.6 \times 10^{-19})$. Since $1/1.6 = 0.625$, this is {{1}} (choose the correct value). The SI unit of electric charge is the {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Inverting $1.6$ gives $0.625$, so $0.625 \times 10^{19} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}$ electrons. Charge is measured in coulombs, symbol C.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: An object has $5.0 \times 10^{12}$ more electrons than protons. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, find the magnitude and sign of the net charge.
questions.solution: The net charge magnitude is $|Q| = (5.0 \times 10^{12})(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = $ {{1}} $\times 10^{-7}\ \text{C}$. Because electrons are in excess, the sign of the net charge is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Multiplying $5.0 \times 1.6 = 8.0$ and $10^{12} \times 10^{-19} = 10^{-7}$ gives $8.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C}$. Excess electrons make the charge negative.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A negatively charged rod is brought near, but does not touch, an isolated neutral metal bar. Describe the induced charges on the two ends of the bar.
questions.solution: The rod repels the bar's free electrons toward the far end, so the near end (closer to the rod) becomes {{1}} and the far end becomes {{2}}. The bar as a whole remains {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Electrons are pushed away from the negative rod, leaving the near end electron-poor (positive) and crowding the far end (negative). No charge enters or leaves, so the bar stays neutral overall.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers

questions.stem: A magnesium ion $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ has lost two electrons. Taking $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, find its net charge and identify whether it is a cation or an anion.
questions.solution: Losing two electrons gives a net charge of $+2e = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = $ {{1}} $\times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. A positively charged ion is called a {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Two missing electrons give $+2e = 3.2 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. A positive ion is a cation; a negative ion would be an anion.
questions.source: in the style of Tipler, Physics for Scientists and Engineers
